[[sn:commons:Category:Sippōryū-ji]][[ja:七宝瀧寺]][[zh:七寶瀧寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shippōryū-ji hondō 2008.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182}}|prefecture={{ill|Izumisano|WD=Q852018}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.3385|N|135.387389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946}}|deity={{ill|Acala|WD=Q337624}}|part_of={{ill|En no Gyōja Reiseki Fudasho|WD=Q11070136}}; {{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581}}; {{ill|Q65249978|WD=Q65249978}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10864580]] Wikidata name: Shippōryū-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Shipporyuji Temple]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Shipporyuji Temple|Shipporyuji Temple]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japan temple infobox | Name = Shippōryū-ji (七宝瀧寺) | image = [[Image:Shippōryū-ji hondō 2008.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall | Location = [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Ōki 8, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|20|18.6|N|135|23|14.6|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shippōryū-ji}} | Mountain name = {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] Inunaki Sect | Rank = Grand Head Temple | Principal image = [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] | Founded = 661 CE (Empress Saimei's reign) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Cultural properties = Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō and attendants; Kurikara sword with gold inlay inscription "Kuniyoshi"; Osaka Prefectural Cultural Property
Inunakisan designated as an Osaka Prefectural Scenic Site
Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]] practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of [[:en:Katsuragi 28 Shuku|Katsuragi 28 Shuku]], a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Treasures === * Manuscripts and artifacts related to Shugendō practices * Historical documents from the Muromachi and Edo periods == Access == ; By Train * Approximately 10 km from [[:en:Hineno Station|Hineno Station]] (JR Hanwa Line) ; By Road * Accessible via Osaka Prefectural Route 62 and other local roads. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Hineno Shrine|ja|日根野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hineno Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Other Shugendō pilgrimage spots in the region. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Website of Shippōryū-ji] * [https://osaka-info.jp/page/inunakisanshipporyuji Osaka Info - Shippōryū-ji] ==Gallery== File:Asashiro-jinja03s3200.jpg|The main hall and Inari shrine (Inari [[:en:Myōjin|Omyojin]]/[[Dakini]]) of {{ill|Enryu-ji|lt=Enryu-ji|lt=Enryuji|ja|円隆寺 (舞鶴市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryuji|qq=}} File:七宝瀧寺鎮守堂_泉佐野市_2013.11.23_-_panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}}・{{ill|Sanbo Kojin|ja|三宝荒神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbo Kojin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}（{{ill|Shipporyuji Temple|ja|七宝瀧寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shipporyuji Temple|qq=}}） File:観心寺にて_鎮守堂(訶梨帝母天堂)_2013.3.15_-_panoramio.jpg|[[:en:Hariti|Hariti]] Hall（[[:en:Kanshin-ji|Kanshin-ji]]） File:Gahaishizan_Mandaraji_05.JPG|Chinju-dō、[[Dō (architecture)|Dō]]、[[Homa (ritual)|Homa]]（{{ill|Mandaraji|lt=Mandaraji|lt=Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|ja|曼荼羅寺 (善通寺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|qq=}}） File:Makisan_Chofukuji_07.JPG|{{ill|Sannō Gongen|lt=Sannō Gongen|lt=Sanno Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanno Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Hall ({{ill|Choufukuji Temple (Mimasaka City)|lt=Makiyama Hannyuin Chou Temple|ja|長福寺 (美作市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choufukuji Temple (Mimasaka City)|qq=}}) ==See Also== {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|七宝瀧寺|version=104668674|comment=Imported full ja history}} Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]] practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of [[:en:Katsuragi 28 Shuku|Katsuragi 28 Shuku]], a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay [[sn:commons:Category:Sippōryū-ji]][[ja:七宝瀧寺]][[zh:七寶瀧寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shippōryū-ji hondō 2008.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182}}|prefecture={{ill|Izumisano|WD=Q852018}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.3385|N|135.387389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946}}|deity={{ill|Acala|WD=Q337624}}|part_of={{ill|En no Gyōja Reiseki Fudasho|WD=Q11070136}}; {{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581}}; {{ill|Q65249978|WD=Q65249978}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10864580]] Wikidata name: Shippōryū-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japan temple infobox | Name = Shippōryū-ji (七宝瀧寺) | image = [[Image:Shippōryū-ji hondō 2008.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall | Location = [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Ōki 8, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|20|18.6|N|135|23|14.6|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shippōryū-ji}} | Mountain name = {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] Inunaki Sect | Rank = Grand Head Temple | Principal image = [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] | Founded = 661 CE (Empress Saimei's reign) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Cultural properties = Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō and attendants; Kurikara sword with gold inlay inscription "Kuniyoshi"; Osaka Prefectural Cultural Property
Inunakisan designated as an Osaka Prefectural Scenic Site
Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]] practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of [[:en:Katsuragi 28 Shuku|Katsuragi 28 Shuku]], a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Treasures === * Manuscripts and artifacts related to Shugendō practices * Historical documents from the Muromachi and Edo periods == Access == ; By Train * Approximately 10 km from [[:en:Hineno Station|Hineno Station]] (JR Hanwa Line) ; By Road * Accessible via Osaka Prefectural Route 62 and other local roads. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Hineno Shrine|ja|日根野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hineno Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Other Shugendō pilgrimage spots in the region. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Website of Shippōryū-ji] * [https://osaka-info.jp/page/inunakisanshipporyuji Osaka Info - Shippōryū-ji] ==Gallery== File:Asashiro-jinja03s3200.jpg|The main hall and Inari shrine (Inari [[:en:Myōjin|Omyojin]]/[[Dakini]]) of {{ill|Enryu-ji|lt=Enryu-ji|lt=Enryuji|ja|円隆寺 (舞鶴市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryuji|qq=}} File:七宝瀧寺鎮守堂_泉佐野市_2013.11.23_-_panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}}・{{ill|Sanbo Kojin|ja|三宝荒神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbo Kojin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}（{{ill|Shipporyuji Temple|ja|七宝瀧寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shipporyuji Temple|qq=}}） File:観心寺にて_鎮守堂(訶梨帝母天堂)_2013.3.15_-_panoramio.jpg|[[:en:Hariti|Hariti]] Hall（[[:en:Kanshin-ji|Kanshin-ji]]） File:Gahaishizan_Mandaraji_05.JPG|Chinju-dō、[[Dō (architecture)|Dō]]、[[Homa (ritual)|Homa]]（{{ill|Mandaraji|lt=Mandaraji|lt=Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|ja|曼荼羅寺 (善通寺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|qq=}}） File:Makisan_Chofukuji_07.JPG|{{ill|Sannō Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]] practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of [[:en:Katsuragi 28 Shuku|Katsuragi 28 Shuku]], a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Shipporyuji Temple]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Shipporyuji Temple|Shipporyuji Temple]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japan temple infobox | Name = Shippōryū-ji (七宝瀧寺) | image = [[Image:Shippōryū-ji hondō 2008.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall | Location = [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Ōki 8, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|20|18.6|N|135|23|14.6|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shippōryū-ji}} | Mountain name = {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] Inunaki Sect | Rank = Grand Head Temple | Principal image = [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] | Founded = 661 CE (Empress Saimei's reign) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Cultural properties = Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō and attendants; Kurikara sword with gold inlay inscription "Kuniyoshi"; Osaka Prefectural Cultural Property
Inunakisan designated as an Osaka Prefectural Scenic Site
Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]] practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of [[:en:Katsuragi 28 Shuku|Katsuragi 28 Shuku]], a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Treasures === * Manuscripts and artifacts related to Shugendō practices * Historical documents from the Muromachi and Edo periods == Access == ; By Train * Approximately 10 km from [[:en:Hineno Station|Hineno Station]] (JR Hanwa Line) ; By Road * Accessible via Osaka Prefectural Route 62 and other local roads. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Hineno Shrine|ja|日根野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hineno Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Other Shugendō pilgrimage spots in the region. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Website of Shippōryū-ji] * [https://osaka-info.jp/page/inunakisanshipporyuji Osaka Info - Shippōryū-ji] ==Gallery== File:Asashiro-jinja03s3200.jpg|The main hall and Inari shrine (Inari [[:en:Myōjin|Omyojin]]/[[Dakini]]) of {{ill|Enryu-ji|lt=Enryu-ji|lt=Enryuji|ja|円隆寺 (舞鶴市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryuji|qq=}} File:七宝瀧寺鎮守堂_泉佐野市_2013.11.23_-_panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}}・{{ill|Sanbo Kojin|ja|三宝荒神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbo Kojin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}（{{ill|Shipporyuji Temple|ja|七宝瀧寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shipporyuji Temple|qq=}}） File:観心寺にて_鎮守堂(訶梨帝母天堂)_2013.3.15_-_panoramio.jpg|[[:en:Hariti|Hariti]] Hall（[[:en:Kanshin-ji|Kanshin-ji]]） File:Gahaishizan_Mandaraji_05.JPG|Chinju-dō、[[Dō (architecture)|Dō]]、[[Homa (ritual)|Homa]]（{{ill|Mandaraji|lt=Mandaraji|lt=Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|ja|曼荼羅寺 (善通寺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|qq=}}） File:Makisan_Chofukuji_07.JPG|{{ill|Sannō Gongen|lt=Sannō Gongen|lt=Sanno Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanno Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Hall ({{ill|Choufukuji Temple (Mimasaka City)|lt=Makiyama Hannyuin Chou and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Inunakisan designated as an Osaka Prefectural Scenic Site
Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]] practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of [[:en:Katsuragi 28 Shuku|Katsuragi 28 Shuku]], a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Treasures === * Manuscripts and artifacts related to Shugendō practices * Historical documents from the Muromachi and Edo periods == Access == ; By Train * Approximately 10 km from [[:en:Hineno Station|Hineno Station]] (JR Hanwa Line) ; By Road * Accessible via Osaka Prefectural Route 62 and other local roads. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Hineno Shrine|ja|日根野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hineno Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Other Shugendō pilgrimage spots in the region. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [[commons:Category:Sippōryū-ji]][[ja:七宝瀧寺]][[zh:七寶瀧寺]] [[sn:commons:Category:Sippōryū-ji]][[ja:七宝瀧寺]][[zh:七寶瀧寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shippōryū-ji hondō 2008.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182}}|prefecture={{ill|Izumisano|WD=Q852018}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.3385|N|135.387389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946}}|deity={{ill|Acala|WD=Q337624}}|part_of={{ill|En no Gyōja Reiseki Fudasho|WD=Q11070136}}; {{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581}}; {{ill|Q65249978|WD=Q65249978}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10864580]] Wikidata name: Shippōryū-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japan temple infobox | Name = Shippōryū-ji (七宝瀧寺) | image = [[Image:Shippōryū-ji hondō 2008.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall | Location = [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Ōki 8, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|20|18.6|N|135|23|14.6|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shippōryū-ji}} | Mountain name = {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] Inunaki Sect | Rank = Grand Head Temple | Principal image = [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] | Founded = 661 CE (Empress Saimei's reign) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Cultural properties = Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō and attendants; Kurikara sword with gold inlay inscription "Kuniyoshi"; Osaka Prefectural Cultural Property
Inunakisan designated as an Osaka Prefectural Scenic Site
Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]] practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of [[:en:Katsuragi 28 Shuku|Katsuragi 28 Shuku]], a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Treasures === * Manuscripts and artifacts related to Shugendō practices * Historical documents from the Muromachi and Edo periods == Access == ; By Train * Approximately 10 km from [[:en:Hineno Station|Hineno Station]] (JR Hanwa Line) ; By Road * Accessible via Osaka Prefectural Route 62 and other local roads. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Hineno Shrine|ja|日根野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hineno Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Other Shugendō pilgrimage spots in the region. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Website of Shippōryū-ji] * [https://osaka-info.jp/page/inunakisanshipporyuji Osaka Info - Shippōryū-ji] ==Gallery== File:Asashiro-jinja03s3200.jpg|The main hall and Inari shrine (Inari [[:en:Myōjin|Omyojin]]/[[Dakini]]) of {{ill|Enryu-ji|lt=Enryu-ji|lt=Enryuji|ja|円隆寺 (舞鶴市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryuji|qq=}} File:七宝瀧寺鎮守堂_泉佐野市_2013.11.23_-_panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}}・{{ill|Sanbo Kojin|ja|三宝荒神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbo Kojin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}（{{ill|Shipporyuji Temple|ja|七宝瀧寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shipporyuji Temple|qq=}}） File:観心寺にて_鎮守堂(訶梨帝母天堂)_2013.3.15_-_panoramio.jpg|[[:en:Hariti|Hariti]] Hall（[[:en:Kanshin-ji|Kanshin-ji]]） File:Gahaishizan_Mandaraji_05.JPG|Chinju-dō、[[Dō (architecture)|Dō]]、[[Homa (ritual)|Homa]]（{{ill|Mandaraji|lt=Mandaraji|lt=Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|ja|曼荼羅寺 (善通寺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|qq=}}） File:Makisan_Chofukuji_07.JPG|{{ill|Sannō It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Shipporyuji Temple]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Shipporyuji Temple|Shipporyuji Temple]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japan temple infobox | Name = Shippōryū-ji (七宝瀧寺) | image = [[Image:Shippōryū-ji hondō 2008.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall | Location = [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Ōki 8, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|20|18.6|N|135|23|14.6|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shippōryū-ji}} | Mountain name = {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] Inunaki Sect | Rank = Grand Head Temple | Principal image = [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] | Founded = 661 CE (Empress Saimei's reign) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Cultural properties = Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō and attendants; Kurikara sword with gold inlay inscription "Kuniyoshi"; Osaka Prefectural Cultural Property
Inunakisan designated as an Osaka Prefectural Scenic Site
Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]] practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of [[:en:Katsuragi 28 Shuku|Katsuragi 28 Shuku]], a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Treasures === * Manuscripts and artifacts related to Shugendō practices * Historical documents from the Muromachi and Edo periods == Access == ; By Train * Approximately 10 km from [[:en:Hineno Station|Hineno Station]] (JR Hanwa Line) ; By Road * Accessible via Osaka Prefectural Route 62 and other local roads. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Hineno Shrine|ja|日根野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hineno Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Other Shugendō pilgrimage spots in the region. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Website of Shippōryū-ji] * [https://osaka-info.jp/page/inunakisanshipporyuji Osaka Info - Shippōryū-ji] ==Gallery== File:Asashiro-jinja03s3200.jpg|The main hall and Inari shrine (Inari [[:en:Myōjin|Omyojin]]/[[Dakini]]) of {{ill|Enryu-ji|lt=Enryu-ji|lt=Enryuji|ja|円隆寺 (舞鶴市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryuji|qq=}} File:七宝瀧寺鎮守堂_泉佐野市_2013.11.23_-_panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}}・{{ill|Sanbo Kojin|ja|三宝荒神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbo Kojin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}（{{ill|Shipporyuji Temple|ja|七宝瀧寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shipporyuji Temple|qq=}}） File:観心寺にて_鎮守堂(訶梨帝母天堂)_2013.3.15_-_panoramio.jpg|[[:en:Hariti|Hariti]] Hall（[[:en:Kanshin-ji|Kanshin-ji]]） File:Gahaishizan_Mandaraji_05.JPG|Chinju-dō、[[Dō (architecture)|Dō]]、[[Homa (ritual)|Homa]]（{{ill|Mandaraji|lt=Mandaraji|lt=Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|ja|曼荼羅寺 (善通寺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|qq=}}） File:Makisan_Chofukuji_07.JPG|{{ill|Sannō Gongen|lt=Sannō Gongen|lt=Sanno Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanno Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Hall ({{ill|Choufukuji Temple (Mimasaka City)|lt=Makiyama Hannyuin Chou Temple|ja|長福寺 (美作市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choufukuji Temple (Mimasaka City)|qq=}}) ==See Also== {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|七宝瀧寺|version=104668674|comment=Imported full ja history}} Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]] practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of [[:en:Katsuragi 28 Shuku|Katsuragi 28 Shuku]], a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Treasures === * Manuscripts and artifacts related to Shugendō practices * Historical documents from the Muromachi and Edo periods == Access == ; By Train * Approximately 10 km from [[:en:Hineno Station|Hineno Station]] (JR Hanwa Line) ; By Road * Accessible via Osaka Prefectural Route 62 and other local roads. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Hineno Shrine|ja|日根野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hineno Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Other Shugendō pilgrimage spots in the region. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [[co:commons:Category:Sippōryū-ji]][[ja:七宝瀧寺]][[zh:七寶瀧寺]] [[co:commons:Category:Sippōryū-ji]][[ja:七宝瀧寺]][[zh:七寶瀧寺]] [[sn:commons:Category:Sippōryū-ji]][[ja:七宝瀧寺]][[zh:七寶瀧寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shippōryū-ji hondō 2008.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182}}|prefecture={{ill|Izumisano|WD=Q852018}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.3385|N|135.387389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946}}|deity={{ill|Acala|WD=Q337624}}|part_of={{ill|En no Gyōja Reiseki Fudasho|WD=Q11070136}}; {{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581}}; {{ill|Q65249978|WD=Q65249978}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10864580]] Wikidata name: Shippōryū-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. Main Hall | Location = [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Ōki 8, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|20|18.6|N|135|23|14.6|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shippōryū-ji}} | Mountain name = {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] Inunaki Sect | Rank = Grand Head Temple | Principal image = [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] | Founded = 661 CE (Empress Saimei's reign) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Cultural properties = Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō and attendants; Kurikara sword with gold inlay inscription "Kuniyoshi"; Osaka Prefectural Cultural Property
Inunakisan designated as an Osaka Prefectural Scenic Site
Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]] practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of [[:en:Katsuragi 28 Shuku|Katsuragi 28 Shuku]], a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Treasures === * Manuscripts and artifacts related to Shugendō practices * Historical documents from the Muromachi and Edo periods == Access == ; By Train * Approximately 10 km from [[:en:Hineno Station|Hineno Station]] (JR Hanwa Line) ; By Road * Accessible via Osaka Prefectural Route 62 and other local roads. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Hineno Shrine|ja|日根野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hineno Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Other Shugendō pilgrimage spots in the region. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Website of Shippōryū-ji] * [https://osaka-info.jp/page/inunakisanshipporyuji Osaka Info - Shippōryū-ji] ==Gallery== File:Asashiro-jinja03s3200.jpg|The main hall and Inari shrine (Inari [[:en:Myōjin|Omyojin]]/[[Dakini]]) of {{ill|Enryu-ji|lt=Enryu-ji|lt=Enryuji|ja|円隆寺 (舞鶴市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryuji|qq=}} File:七宝瀧寺鎮守堂_泉佐野市_2013.11.23_-_panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}}・{{ill|Sanbo Kojin|ja|三宝荒神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbo Kojin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}（{{ill|Shipporyuji Temple|ja|七宝瀧寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shipporyuji Temple|qq=}}） (ritual)|Homa]]（{{ill|Mandaraji|lt=Mandaraji|lt=Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|ja|曼荼羅寺 (善通寺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|qq=}}） File:Makisan_Chofukuji_07.JPG|{{ill|Sannō It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japan temple infobox | Name = Shippōryū-ji (七宝瀧寺) | image = [[Image:Shippōryū-ji hondō 2008.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall | Location = [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Ōki 8, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|20|18.6|N|135|23|14.6|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shippōryū-ji}} | Mountain name = {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} | Sect = [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]] Inunaki Sect | Rank = Grand Head Temple | Principal image = [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] | Founded = 661 CE (Empress Saimei's reign) | Founder = [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] | Cultural properties = Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō and attendants; Kurikara sword with gold inlay inscription "Kuniyoshi"; Osaka Prefectural Cultural Property
Inunakisan designated as an Osaka Prefectural Scenic Site
Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in [[:en:Izumisano|Izumisano]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]] practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]] founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of [[:en:Katsuragi 28 Shuku|Katsuragi 28 Shuku]], a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Treasures === * Manuscripts and artifacts related to Shugendō practices * Historical documents from the Muromachi and Edo periods == Access == ; By Train * Approximately 10 km from [[:en:Hineno Station|Hineno Station]] (JR Hanwa Line) ; By Road * Accessible via Osaka Prefectural Route 62 and other local roads. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Hineno Shrine|ja|日根野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hineno Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Other Shugendō pilgrimage spots in the region. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Website of Shippōryū-ji] * [https://osaka-info.jp/page/inunakisanshipporyuji Osaka Info - Shippōryū-ji] ==Gallery== File:Asashiro-jinja03s3200.jpg|The main hall and Inari shrine (Inari [[:en:Myōjin|Omyojin]]/[[Dakini]]) of {{ill|Enryu-ji|lt=Enryu-ji|lt=Enryuji|ja|円隆寺 (舞鶴市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryuji|qq=}} File:七宝瀧寺鎮守堂_泉佐野市_2013.11.23_-_panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}}・{{ill|Sanbo Kojin|ja|三宝荒神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbo Kojin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}（{{ill|Shipporyuji Temple|ja|七宝瀧寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shipporyuji Temple|qq=}}） File:観心寺にて_鎮守堂(訶梨帝母天堂)_2013.3.15_-_panoramio.jpg|[[:en:Hariti|Hariti]] Hall（[[:en:Kanshin-ji|Kanshin-ji]]） File:Gahaishizan_Mandaraji_05.JPG|Chinju-dō、[[Dō (architecture)|Dō]]、[[Homa (ritual)|Homa]]（{{ill|Mandaraji|lt=Mandaraji|lt=Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|ja|曼荼羅寺 (善通寺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|qq=}}） File:Makisan_Chofukuji_07.JPG|{{ill|Sannō Gongen|lt=Sannō Gongen|lt=Sanno Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanno Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Hall ({{ill|Choufukuji Temple (Mimasaka City)|lt=Makiyama Hannyuin Chou Temple|ja|長福寺 (美作市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choufukuji Temple (Mimasaka City)|qq=}}) ==See Also== {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki Main Hall | Location = {{ill|Izumisano|en|Izumisano|WD=Q852018|lt=Izumisano}}, Ōki 8, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|20|18.6|N|135|23|14.6|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shippōryū-ji}} | Mountain name = {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} Inunaki Sect | Rank = Grand Head Temple | Principal image = {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}} | Founded = 661 CE (Empress Saimei's reign) | Founder = {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja}} | Cultural properties = Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō and attendants; Kurikara sword with gold inlay inscription "Kuniyoshi"; Osaka Prefectural Cultural Property
Inunakisan designated as an Osaka Prefectural Scenic Site
Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in {{ill|Izumisano|en|Izumisano|WD=Q852018|lt=Izumisano}}, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}}. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō}} practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja}} founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of {{ill|Katsuragi 28 Shuku|en|Katsuragi 28 Shuku|WD=Q11620888|lt=Katsuragi 28 Shuku}}, a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Treasures === * Manuscripts and artifacts related to Shugendō practices * Historical documents from the Muromachi and Edo periods == Access == ; By Train * Approximately 10 km from {{ill|Hineno Station|en|Hineno Station|WD=Q856937|lt=Hineno Station}} (JR Hanwa Line) ; By Road * Accessible via Osaka Prefectural Route 62 and other local roads. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Hineno [[co:commons:Category:Sippōryū-ji]][[ja:七宝瀧寺]][[zh:七寶瀧寺]] [[co:commons:Category:Sippōryū-ji]][[ja:七宝瀧寺]][[zh:七寶瀧寺]] [[sn:commons:Category:Sippōryū-ji]][[ja:七宝瀧寺]][[zh:七寶瀧寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shippōryū-ji hondō 2008.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182}}|prefecture={{ill|Izumisano|WD=Q852018}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.3385|N|135.387389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946}}|deity={{ill|Acala|WD=Q337624}}|part_of={{ill|En no Gyōja Reiseki Fudasho|WD=Q11070136}}; {{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581}}; {{ill|Q65249978|WD=Q65249978}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}}} {{afc comment|1=to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Shipporyuji Temple]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Shipporyuji Temple|Shipporyuji Temple]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japan temple infobox | Name = Shippōryū-ji (七宝瀧寺) | image = [[Image:Shippōryū-ji hondō 2008.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall | Location = {{ill|Izumisano|en|Izumisano|WD=Q852018|lt=Izumisano}}, Ōki 8, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|20|18.6|N|135|23|14.6|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shippōryū-ji}} | Mountain name = {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} Inunaki Sect | Rank = Grand Head Temple | Principal image = {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}} | Founded = 661 CE (Empress Saimei's reign) | Founder = {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja}} | Cultural properties = Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō and attendants; Kurikara sword with gold inlay inscription "Kuniyoshi"; Osaka Prefectural Cultural Property
Inunakisan designated as an Osaka Prefectural Scenic Site
Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in {{ill|Izumisano|en|Izumisano|WD=Q852018|lt=Izumisano}}, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}}. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō}} practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja}} founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of {{ill|Katsuragi 28 Shuku|en|Katsuragi 28 Shuku|WD=Q11620888|lt=Katsuragi 28 Shuku}}, a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Treasures === * Manuscripts and artifacts related to Shugendō practices * Historical documents from the Muromachi and Edo periods == Access == ; By Train * Approximately 10 km from {{ill|Hineno Station|en|Hineno Station|WD=Q856937|lt=Hineno Station}} (JR Hanwa Line) ; By Road * Accessible via Osaka Prefectural Route 62 and other local roads. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Hineno Shrine|ja|日根野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hineno Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Other Shugendō pilgrimage spots in the region. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Website of Shippōryū-ji] * [https://osaka-info.jp/page/inunakisanshipporyuji Osaka Info - Shippōryū-ji] ==Gallery== File:Asashiro-jinja03s3200.jpg|The main hall and Inari shrine (Inari {{ill|Myōjin|en|Myōjin|WD=Q5369590|lt=Omyojin}}/[[Dakini]]) of {{ill|Enryu-ji|lt=Enryu-ji|lt=Enryuji|ja|円隆寺 (舞鶴市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryuji|qq=}} File:七宝瀧寺鎮守堂_泉佐野市_2013.11.23_-_panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}}・{{ill|Sanbo Kojin|ja|三宝荒神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbo Kojin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}（{{ill|Shipporyuji Temple|ja|七宝瀧寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shipporyuji Temple|qq=}}） File:観心寺にて_鎮守堂(訶梨帝母天堂)_2013.3.15_-_panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Hariti|en|Hariti|WD=Q1353280|lt=Hariti}} Hall（{{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji}}） File:Gahaishizan_Mandaraji_05.JPG|Chinju-dō、[[Dō (architecture)|Dō]]、[[Homa (ritual)|Homa]]（{{ill|Mandaraji|lt=Mandaraji|lt=Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|ja|曼荼羅寺 (善通寺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|qq=}}） File:Makisan_Chofukuji_07.JPG|{{ill|Sannō Gongen|lt=Sannō Gongen|lt=Sanno Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanno Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Hall ({{ill|Choufukuji Temple (Mimasaka City)|lt=Makiyama Hannyuin Chou Temple|ja|長福寺 (美作市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choufukuji Temple (Mimasaka City)|qq=}}) ==See Also== {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please
