[[co:commons:Category:Sippōryū-ji]][[ja:七宝瀧寺]][[zh:七寶瀧寺]] [[co:commons:Category:Sippōryū-ji]][[ja:七宝瀧寺]][[zh:七寶瀧寺]] [[sn:commons:Category:Sippōryū-ji]][[ja:七宝瀧寺]][[zh:七寶瀧寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shippōryū-ji hondō 2008.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182}}|prefecture={{ill|Izumisano|WD=Q852018}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.3385|N|135.387389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946}}|deity={{ill|Acala|WD=Q337624}}|part_of={{ill|En no Gyōja Reiseki Fudasho|WD=Q11070136}}; {{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581}}; {{ill|Q65249978|WD=Q65249978}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}}} {{Japan temple infobox | Name = Shippōryū-ji (七宝瀧寺) | image = [[Image:Shippōryū-ji hondō 2008.jpg|280px]]
Main Hall | Location = {{ill|Izumisano|en|Izumisano|WD=Q852018|lt=Izumisano}}, Ōki 8, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|20|18.6|N|135|23|14.6|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shippōryū-ji}} | Mountain name = {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism}} Inunaki Sect | Rank = Grand Head Temple | Principal image = {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}} | Founded = 661 CE (Empress Saimei's reign) | Founder = {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja}} | Cultural properties = Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō and attendants; Kurikara sword with gold inlay inscription "Kuniyoshi"; Osaka Prefectural Cultural Property
Inunakisan designated as an Osaka Prefectural Scenic Site
Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in {{ill|Izumisano|en|Izumisano|WD=Q852018|lt=Izumisano}}, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}}. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō}} practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja}} founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of {{ill|Katsuragi 28 Shuku|en|Katsuragi 28 Shuku|WD=Q11620888|lt=Katsuragi 28 Shuku}}, a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Inunakisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Treasures === * Manuscripts and artifacts related to Shugendō practices * Historical documents from the Muromachi and Edo periods == Access == ; By Train * Approximately 10 km from {{ill|Hineno Station|en|Hineno Station|WD=Q856937|lt=Hineno Station}} (JR Hanwa Line) ; By Road * Accessible via Osaka Prefectural Route 62 and other local roads. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Hineno Shrine|ja|日根野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hineno Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Other Shugendō pilgrimage spots in the region. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Website of Shippōryū-ji] * [https://osaka-info.jp/page/inunakisanshipporyuji Osaka Info - Shippōryū-ji] ==Gallery== File:Asashiro-jinja03s3200.jpg|The main hall and Inari shrine (Inari {{ill|Myōjin|en|Myōjin|WD=Q5369590|lt=Omyojin}}/[[Dakini]]) of {{ill|Enryu-ji|lt=Enryu-ji|lt=Enryuji|ja|円隆寺 (舞鶴市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryuji|qq=}} File:七宝瀧寺鎮守堂_泉佐野市_2013.11.23_-_panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}}・{{ill|Sanbo Kojin|ja|三宝荒神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbo Kojin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}（{{ill|Shipporyuji Temple|ja|七宝瀧寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shipporyuji Temple|qq=}}） File:観心寺にて_鎮守堂(訶梨帝母天堂)_2013.3.15_-_panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Hariti|en|Hariti|WD=Q1353280|lt=Hariti}} Hall（{{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji}}） File:Gahaishizan_Mandaraji_05.JPG|Chinju-dō、[[Dō (architecture)|Dō]]、[[Homa (ritual)|Homa]]（{{ill|Mandaraji|lt=Mandaraji|lt=Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|ja|曼荼羅寺 (善通寺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|qq=}}） File:Makisan_Chofukuji_07.JPG|{{ill|Sannō Gongen|lt=Sannō Gongen|lt=Sanno Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanno Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Hall ({{ill|Choufukuji Temple (Mimasaka City)|lt=Makiyama Hannyuin Chou Temple|ja|長福寺 (美作市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choufukuji Temple (Mimasaka City)|qq=}}) ==See Also== {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|七宝瀧寺|version=104668674|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{everybodywiki link|Shipporyuji Temple}} [[ja:七宝瀧寺]] {{Commons category|Sippōryū-ji}} Inunakisan designated as an Osaka Prefectural Scenic Site
Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in {{ill|Izumisano|en|Izumisano|WD=Q852018|lt=Izumisano|1=Izumisano}}, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Q11571296|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}}. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja|1=En no Gyōja}} founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of {{ill|Katsuragi 28 Shuku|en|Katsuragi 28 Shuku|WD=Q11620888|lt=Katsuragi 28 Shuku|1=Katsuragi 28 Shuku}}, a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Q11571296|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Q11571296|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Treasures === * Manuscripts and artifacts related to Shugendō practices * Historical documents from the Muromachi and Edo periods == Access == ; By Train * Approximately 10 km from {{ill|Hineno Station|en|Hineno Station|WD=Q856937|lt=Hineno Station|1=Hineno Station}} (JR Hanwa Line) ; By Road * Accessible via Osaka Prefectural Route 62 and other local roads. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Hineno Shrine|ja|日根野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hineno Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Other Shugendō pilgrimage spots in the region. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * Main Hall | Location = {{ill|Izumisano|en|Izumisano|WD=Q852018|lt=Izumisano|1=Izumisano}}, Ōki 8, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|20|18.6|N|135|23|14.6|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shippōryū-ji}} | Mountain name = {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Q11571296|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}} Inunaki Sect | Rank = Grand Head Temple | Principal image = {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}} | Founded = 661 CE (Empress Saimei's reign) | Founder = {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja|1=En no Gyōja}} | Cultural properties = Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō and attendants; Kurikara sword with gold inlay inscription "Kuniyoshi"; Osaka Prefectural Cultural Property
Inunakisan designated as an Osaka Prefectural Scenic Site
Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in {{ill|Izumisano|en|Izumisano|WD=Q852018|lt=Izumisano|1=Izumisano}}, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Q11571296|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}}. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja|1=En no Gyōja}} founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of {{ill|Katsuragi 28 Shuku|en|Katsuragi 28 Shuku|WD=Q11620888|lt=Katsuragi 28 Shuku|1=Katsuragi 28 Shuku}}, a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Q11571296|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Q11571296|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Treasures === * Manuscripts and artifacts related to Shugendō practices * Historical documents from the Muromachi and Edo periods == Access == ; By Train * Approximately 10 km from {{ill|Hineno Station|en|Hineno Station|WD=Q856937|lt=Hineno Station|1=Hineno Station}} (JR Hanwa Line) ; By Road * Accessible via Osaka Prefectural Route 62 and other local roads. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Hineno Shrine|ja|日根野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hineno Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Other Shugendō pilgrimage spots in the region. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Website of Shippōryū-ji] * [https://osaka-info.jp/page/inunakisanshipporyuji Osaka Info - Shippōryū-ji] ==Gallery== File:Asashiro-jinja03s3200.jpg|The main hall and Inari shrine (Inari {{ill|Myōjin|en|Myōjin|WD=Q5369590|lt=Omyojin|1=Myōjin}}/[[Dakini]]) of {{ill|Enryu-ji|lt=Enryu-ji|lt=Enryuji|ja|円隆寺 (舞鶴市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryuji|qq=}} File:七宝瀧寺鎮守堂_泉佐野市_2013.11.23_-_panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Chintaku Reifu|ja|鎮宅霊符神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮宅霊符}}・{{ill|Sanbo Kojin|ja|三宝荒神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbo Kojin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}（{{ill|Shipporyuji Temple|ja|七宝瀧寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shipporyuji Temple|qq=}}） File:観心寺にて_鎮守堂(訶梨帝母天堂)_2013.3.15_-_panoramio.jpg|{{ill|Hariti|en|Hariti|WD=Q1353280|lt=Hariti|1=Hariti}} Hall（{{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}}） File:Gahaishizan_Mandaraji_05.JPG|Chinju-dō、[[Dō (architecture)|Dō]]、[[Homa (ritual)|Homa]]（{{ill|Mandaraji|lt=Mandaraji|lt=Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|ja|曼荼羅寺 (善通寺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gahaishizan Mandaraji Temple|qq=}}） File:Makisan_Chofukuji_07.JPG|{{ill|Sannō Gongen|lt=Sannō Gongen|lt=Sanno Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanno During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Q11571296|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural Inunakisan designated as an Osaka Prefectural Scenic Site
Constituent cultural property of the 2019 Japanese Heritage "Landscapes of Medieval Hineno-shō" | Website = [http://www.inunakisan.jp/ Official Site of Shippōryū-ji] }} '''Shippōryū-ji''' (七宝瀧寺) is a Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Shingon Inunaki Sect|ja|真言宗犬鳴派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Inunaki Sect|qq=}}, located in {{ill|Izumisano|en|Izumisano|WD=Q852018|lt=Izumisano|1=Izumisano}}, Osaka Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name is {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Q11571296|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}, and its principal image is {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}}. Shippōryū-ji has been recognized as a central location for Katsuragi {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} practices and holds historical and spiritual significance. == History == The temple is said to have been established in 661 CE, six years before {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja|1=En no Gyōja}} founded Mount Ōmine. Historically, it served as a pivotal hub connecting the routes of {{ill|Katsuragi 28 Shuku|en|Katsuragi 28 Shuku|WD=Q11620888|lt=Katsuragi 28 Shuku|1=Katsuragi 28 Shuku}}, a Shugendō path. In the medieval period, Shippōryū-ji was an integral part of the cultural and spiritual landscape linking Izumi and Kii Provinces. Shippōryū-ji's history includes associations with legends such as the "Dog Crying Legend" (義犬伝説), which inspired the mountain's name, {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Q11571296|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}}. During the Heian period, Emperor Junna named the temple "Shippōryū-ji" following a successful rain prayer. Despite destruction during the Sengoku period, it was rebuilt with support from Toyotomi Hideyoshi and others. By the Edo period, Shippōryū-ji became a prominent Shugendō training site and received additional land donations. The temple continued to thrive until the Meiji Restoration's anti-Buddhist movement and Shugendō prohibition, after which it was revitalized in the late Meiji era. In 1950, Shippōryū-ji established the Inunaki Sect of Shingon Buddhism and resumed its role as a spiritual center for Shugendō. == Buildings and Grounds == The temple complex features both historical and Shugendō-related structures, including: * '''Main Hall''': Houses the hidden image of Kurikara Fudō Myōō, revealed in specific years during special ceremonies. * '''Seven Waterfalls''': Includes training sites like "Ryōkai Falls." * '''Shugendō Training Grounds''': North and south of the Inunaki River. * '''Cultural Properties''': Structures such as the Hondō (Main Hall), Kannon-dō (Kannon Hall), and numerous stone carvings and statues. == Cultural Properties == === Osaka Prefectural Cultural Properties === * Hanging scroll of Fudō Myōō with attendants * Kurikara Sword with gold inlay inscription === Designated Scenic Site === * {{ill|Inunakisan|ja|犬鳴山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inunakisan|1=Q11571296|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11571296}} === Other Cultural
